[Marc Alex Forget]
Sure - no rush. Thanks!

---

[Adam Nelson]
Yeah, let's chat tomorrow about it

---

[Marc Alex Forget]
Hi Adam,

When we met at Ian's place, I heard you talk about Claude Enterprise Plan from Anthropic.

From what I heard it is an excellent tool for analyzing and summarizing meeting notes. 

This is something my team can benefit from. 

While I don't think it join live calls, if it shines at processing uploaded transcripts to extract actionable insights, generate summaries, and draft follow-up emails... will we be in a position to use a Teams feature to transcript and Claude to analyze and summarise meeting notes into the CRM (Salesforce Opp) and JIRA (GSE)?

---

[Adam Nelson]
Adam Nelson assuming they want VMware, yes.  However, there is a nuance to that answer.

Apt Cloud supports VMware Cloud Director natively, but with limited support.  It is a much more complicated implementation because it depends on a few different VMware components (VRops, vRealize, etc).  VMware is prone to issues in not being consistent with their usage data.  All to say, it is possible.  This is how MTC is exposed through Apt Cloud today.  We can expose self-service, but for MTC, we haven't exposed self-service to our customers (too many competing priorities).
CloudStack supports VMware as a hypervisor (at the vSphere API layer) out of the box and it is arguably the best supported hypervisor (from a feature perspective).  This means that the Aptum IaaS layer can support VMware clusters being orchestrated through that implementation.  We can dedicate clusters to customers within the Aptum IaaS layer if we need to.  So a customer would then be able to orchestrate shared resources (on our KVM infra) and also orchestrate their down dedicated VMware resources through the same interface.

items.subject: [SALES HELP] Sales Infrastructure & Cloud Questions
items.body_text:
[Marc Alex Forget]
Product, Marketing and Sales Enablement - Documents - Addendum & Order forms - All Documents

There is not an Order Form specific to colo. The Schedule ''A'' has to be updated to reflect colo services.

---

[Adam Nelson]
Does anyone know where the colo order form is?

 

Martin Tessier was looking

---

[Ian Rae]
It isn’t different however we have partners who are 100% Canadian controlled such as ThinkOn and Hypertec. This is a complex topic we probably should write an FAQ on this. We are able to provide Canadian controlled and operated solutions but not all of our solutions are 100% Canadian. Even if a company is Canadian controlled there is a spectrum of supply chain autonomy from foreign jurisdictions. If you need answers urgently just call me. Meantime we should get a FAQ for the website Marc Alex Forget something to add to the list of website TO Dos?

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So when the customer does directly ask, I don't feel comfortable with anything above. Can we get an official statement or something that I can refer customers to? This seems like a big enough deal that we should have an official stance / statement.

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Question I got from a prospective client:

 

How is that any different from Microsoft Canada? They are a Canadian entity with Canadian data centers, with us based ownership

---

[Martin Tessier]
I spoke to Melvin about that already.. It's a legal thing so we went to Sarah and here's her answer 

 

Jurisdiction:

If the contract is with our Canadian entity, it will generally be governed by Canadian law (federal or provincial, depending on the contract).
U.S. laws like the CLOUD Act technically only apply to U.S.-incorporated entities. A Canadian company is generally not directly subject, unless we have U.S.-based systems storing the data.

Data residency / access:

If the customer wants all data to stay in Canada, the clause should focus on:

Storage in Canadian servers.
Disclosure only as required under Canadian law.

Practical enforceability:

Aptum cannot promise to ignore legal obligations, but we can contractually promise notification and limited access.
Typical Canadian clauses include phrases like: “Data will not be transferred outside Canada without consent” or “Vendor will notify Customer of any government requests under Canadian law.”

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
I pinged Melvin

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Are we exempt from the cloud act

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
The cloud act is the primary concern

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So saying we meet regional data laws doesn't mean we are excluded from the US cloud act since we have an American parent company

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
So does Microsoft.... Unless the us governments demands customer data from them.... Then it doesn't matter where it is... That's what people are worried about

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
I've shared that with a customer before

---

[Marko Andjelkovic]
Aptum complies with all applicable data privacy regulations in the regions where we operate. For instance, in the EU, Aptum is in scope for GDPR as a data subprocessor-even though we don’t manage or access customer data directly. We demonstrate our compliance through our Data Processor Addendum, which outlines our obligations as a data subprocessor.

---

[Patrick Wolthausen]
Do we have anything official about our Canadian data sovereignty? I realise we are a Canadian company with Canadian data centers, but I've heard concerns more than once about our parent company being American

items.subject: Your Amazon Web Services Free Tier expires soon
items.body_text:
[Amazon Web Services]
You don't often get email from no-reply@amazonaws.com. Learn why this is important 

 

Hello,

Read carefully and take action to prevent unwanted charges.

The 12-month Amazon Web Services Free Tier period associated with your Amazon Web Services account 195275671096 will expire on March 31, 2026. If no action is taken, your resources will continue to run, and you’ll be automatically billed for any active resources when the 12-month Free Tier period ends.

We strongly advise that you sign in and review your Amazon Web Services Billing & Cost Management Dashboard to locate any active resources on your account that you no longer need. Even if you aren’t using your Amazon Web Services account or have closed the account, it’s possible that you still have active resources.

1. Go to your Billing Dashboard to see the line items by region for each service contributing to your Free Tier usage for the month.
Tip: Select each service or the ‘Expand All’ option to view all active services by region.

2. If you no longer need the resources, terminate them to prevent unwanted charges.

3. Open the Management Console, select the region in the navigation bar where you have any unwanted resources. Enter each service name in the search bar to open its dashboard. Terminate any unwanted resources. Please refer to this guide for detailed steps.
Note: Remember to terminate unwanted resources for each region. Terminating resources in one region will not lead to termination of those resources in other regions.

4. Monitor your Free Tier expiration. Once your short-term trials or 12-month Free Tier period ends, you’ll be charged standard, pay-as-you-go service rates for any active resources.

Sincerely,

Amazon Web Services

items.subject: See how your Professional Services model compares
items.body_text:
[TSIA]
Assess, benchmark, and prioritize with confidence
 

 View this email in your browser 

Hi Adam,
Most Professional Services leaders feel the pressure.
Utilization targets are harder to hit.
Margins are under scrutiny.
Customers expect outcomes, not effort.
But very few can answer this with confidence: Where does our Professional Services model actually break in the AI era?
Not all delivery models respond to AI the same way.

Benchmark your organization 

Some services become ROI engines. Others quietly turn into profit leaks as productivity rises and billable effort falls. The challenge is visibility.

Without clear benchmarks, many PS organizations:
Overinvest in labor-heavy services that no longer scale.
Defend utilization models that weaken as AI accelerates delivery.
Miss where outcome ownership should replace hours-based pricing.This is where leading Professional Services organizations separate instinct from insight.
They use industry data to understand:
Which services remain resilient as productivity accelerates.
How their delivery model compares to peers.
Where to adapt before revenue gaps widen.
The leaders who diagnose early have options. The rest are left reacting.
See how TSIA Performance Optimizer helps leaders benchmark and pressure-test their Professional Services strategy, at no cost.

Hi Matthew,
 
Please find the minutes from today’s call below.
 
***These are AI-generated minutes based on the discussion. Please review for accuracy.
 
Meeting Summary
 
AWS Partner Program Changes (Effective April 1st)
The meeting focused on major AWS partner program changes being rolled out, with Gina (TD SYNNEX subject matter expert) presenting a comprehensive overview. Key changes include a new base discount structure, deprecation of several legacy incentives, and new operational requirements. The changes represent a significant overhaul of how AWS incentivizes channel partners, with many updates piling up simultaneously affecting TD SYNNEX's ~4,900 global partners.
Timeline of Changes:
- January 1st: PGI started; PGD and other incentives deprecated
- February 1st: All RPPA changes went into effect
- April 1st: New base benefit, deprecation of technology component incentive, new NCI, deprecation of POD, PSD, and CEI incentives
 
Partner-Based Discount (Primary Focus)
The new 8% flat front-end discount replaces the previous 3% base + 4% technical capability discount structure. This discount applies to non-PPA, non-internal spend, excluding existing accounts and marketplace revenue. TD SYNNEX passes the full discount through to partners — they do not retain any portion.
Status Details:
- New Base Discount: 8% (up from 3% base; previously 3% + 4% technical competency = 7%)
- Existing Accounts (formerly "Shareshift"): Only 2% discount applies to any member account spending >$50,000/month prior to linking
- Existing Account Policy: Currently permanent (no time limit); TD SYNNEX has lobbied AWS to reduce to 1–2 years but AWS has not agreed
- First billing reflection: May bill (for April usage) will be the first to reflect the new 8% discount
- Competency benefits: Financial discount for competency is now absorbed into base rate, but competencies still provide lead generation and increased MDF access
 
Existing Accounts / Shareshift Classification
The $50,000 threshold applies at the individual member account level, not the customer or payer account as a whole. Strategies to address existing account classification were discussed.
Status Details:
- Accounts under $49,999/month at time of linking receive full channel discount
- Accounts at $50,000+ receive only 2% (existing account rate)
- Exception process available (business use case submission), but Gina noted ~80% failure rate
- Acceptable exception reasons: prior reseller went out of business, unique services the new reseller provides that the old one cannot
- Workarounds discussed:
- Modernization into new member accounts (acceptable)
- Pure lift-and-shift to new member account (frowned upon)
- Normalizing spend across member accounts before linking
- Specific Optum fintech customer noted: Has been consuming direct; team to explore business case exception with AWS to classify as base account given Optum's involvement since inception
 
DSA Migration & Timing (Moving to TD SYNNEX)
Optum is planning to move their US legal entity first, with Canada and UK as a secondary phase. The migration process and timing requirements were clarified.
Status Details:
- Process: Submit APN ID + DSA move request by region to AWS; Rupesh to provide email address
- Deadline: DSA move and CTA must be completed before end-user reporting closes (last Friday of the month — approximately March 22–23rd for April 1st effective date)
- Consent to Assign (CTA): TD SYNNEX will prepare a consolidated document with all payer IDs; Optum signs once; then a 10–15 minute call with both distributors' operations teams to transfer root access
- Important: If DSA is not moved in time, neither old nor new distributor receives the discount (street price applies)
- Canada: Rupesh remains account manager for both US and Canada regions; billing currency can be USD or CAD based on preference
- Former aggregator behavior: May attempt to retain the partner; TD SYNNEX noted competitors have dragged their feet in the past
 
New Customer Incentive (NCI)
NCI is a new program replacing POD (Partner Originated Discount), offering an additional 4% discount for 3 years on qualifying new accounts.
Status Details:
- Eligibility: Customer must have spent less than $50,000 in the prior 12 months (~$5,000/month) before linking
- Benefit: Extra 4% discount for 3 years (vs. POD's 5% for 2 years)
- Requirement: Customer must be enrolled in Billing Transfer
- Billing Transfer overview:
- Customer retains root access
- Handshake model (similar to Microsoft Azure CSP)
- Customer cannot see partner or distributor discounts
- Removes consent-to-assign requirement
- Reduces operational complexity and customer hesitation around giving up root
 
Bad Debt / Bankruptcy Protection
Optum raised concerns about customer bankruptcies and recovery options.
Status Details:
- Billing Transfer advantage: Partner can sever the handshake, stopping future liability; AWS holds remaining spend
- Infrastructure as a service is classified as a utility globally — cannot be shut off during bankruptcy protection
- TD SYNNEX process: Documents all spend, identifies legal counsel, petitions AWS for reimbursement on behalf of partners
- TD SYNNEX pays AWS every two weeks regardless of partner/customer payment status
- TD SYNNEX has successfully petitioned AWS to absorb losses in past cases
- TD SYNNEX noted as better positioned to absorb bad debt than individual partners
 
MDF (Market Development Funds)
TD SYNNEX offers an MDF match program tied to AWS MDF allocations.
Status Details:
- AWS MDF: Advanced partners with competency can access up to $50,000/year (typically ~$25,000); AWS covers 50% after proof of performance
- TD SYNNEX Match: Matches what AWS contributes (covering the other 50%)
- Allocation: Annual basis, not quarterly/monthly; based on qualification level and partner activity
- One-off events: Partners can request ad hoc MDF participation with a business case (ROI, cost, timing)
- TD SYNNEX distributes limited AWS funding across ~1,300–1,400 North America partners; more active partners get more access
 
Payment Terms
Payment terms are determined individually based on credit rating and financials.
Status Details:
- Process: TD SYNNEX credit team reviews financials during onboarding to establish credit line and terms
- Typical terms: Net 30 standard; Net 45 likely achievable; Net 90 rare (requires ~$2–4M/month in AWS spend)
- Terms cannot be confirmed in advance — financial review required first
- Christine to follow up offline on credit/payment term specifics
 
✅ Decisions Made
DSA Migration Scope: US legal entity first; Canada and UK as secondary phase
Billing Currency: US dollars preferred for current migration
Deck Sharing: Gina to share the comprehensive partner-facing program deck with the Optum/Matthew team
 
🎯 Action Items

- Provide AWS email address for DSA move request submission
- Prepare consolidated Consent to Assign (CTA) document with all payer IDs
- Schedule operations call with former aggregator for root access transfer
- Follow up on Adam's questions submitted via chat
@Gina
- Share comprehensive partner-facing program deck (covering all PPA, NCI, support plan changes)
- Support business case exception request for Optum's fintech customer (direct-consuming client) to be classified as base account
@Christine

@Matthew / @Adam / @Marc (Optum Team)

- Complete CTA signing
- Evaluate fintech customer migration strategy (business case exception vs. modernization approach)
- Provide financials to TD SYNNEX credit team for payment terms assessment
- Review email sent by Marc on Tuesday covering payment terms and MDF details
 
📊 Updates Shared
TD SYNNEX Team Structure: Rupesh = primary account manager (North America); Gina = programs SME (AWS incentives, PPA, partner-led support); Christine = escalation; Maz = supervisor/escalation; pre-sales technical resource also assigned
Gina's availability note: High demand across many partners; Rupesh coordinates SME engagement as needed
 
📋 Additional Notes
The technical competency discount (previously 4%) has been absorbed into the new 8% base rate — no additional financial discount for holding a competency, though other benefits (leads, MDF access) remain
SPP certification no longer provides an incremental financial discount under the new structure
Billing Transfer is a strategic tool to reduce customer friction around root access and simplifies operational onboarding
All program changes represent a significant simultaneous shift — partners are encouraged to act quickly ahead of April 1st to avoid billing disruptions
 
 

Thanks & Regards

Meeting Summary
 
AWS Partner Program Changes (Effective April 1st)
The meeting focused on major AWS partner program changes being rolled out, with Gina (TD SYNNEX subject matter expert) presenting a comprehensive overview. Key changes include a new base discount structure, deprecation of several legacy incentives, and new operational requirements. The changes represent a significant overhaul of how AWS incentivizes channel partners, with many updates piling up simultaneously affecting TD SYNNEX's ~4,900 global partners.
Timeline of Changes:
- January 1st: PGI started; PGD and other incentives deprecated
- February 1st: All RPPA changes went into effect
- April 1st: New base benefit, deprecation of technology component incentive, new NCI, deprecation of POD, PSD, and CEI incentives
 
Partner-Based Discount (Primary Focus)
The new 8% flat front-end discount replaces the previous 3% base + 4% technical capability discount structure. This discount applies to non-PPA, non-internal spend, excluding existing accounts and marketplace revenue. TD SYNNEX passes the full discount through to partners — they do not retain any portion.
Status Details:
- New Base Discount: 8% (up from 3% base; previously 3% + 4% technical competency = 7%)
- Existing Accounts (formerly "Shareshift"): Only 2% discount applies to any member account spending >$50,000/month prior to linking
- Existing Account Policy: Currently permanent (no time limit); TD SYNNEX has lobbied AWS to reduce to 1–2 years but AWS has not agreed
- First billing reflection: May bill (for April usage) will be the first to reflect the new 8% discount
- Competency benefits: Financial discount for competency is now absorbed into base rate, but competencies still provide lead generation and increased MDF access
 
Existing Accounts / Shareshift Classification
The $50,000 threshold applies at the individual member account level, not the customer or payer account as a whole. Strategies to address existing account classification were discussed.
Status Details:
- Accounts under $49,999/month at time of linking receive full channel discount
- Accounts at $50,000+ receive only 2% (existing account rate)
- Exception process available (business use case submission), but Gina noted ~80% failure rate
- Acceptable exception reasons: prior reseller went out of business, unique services the new reseller provides that the old one cannot
- Workarounds discussed:
- Modernization into new member accounts (acceptable)
- Pure lift-and-shift to new member account (frowned upon)
- Normalizing spend across member accounts before linking
- Specific Optum fintech customer noted: Has been consuming direct; team to explore business case exception with AWS to classify as base account given Optum's involvement since inception
 
DSA Migration & Timing (Moving to TD SYNNEX)
Optum is planning to move their US legal entity first, with Canada and UK as a secondary phase. The migration process and timing requirements were clarified.
Status Details:
- Process: Submit APN ID + DSA move request by region to AWS; Rupesh to provide email address
- Deadline: DSA move and CTA must be completed before end-user reporting closes (last Friday of the month — approximately March 22–23rd for April 1st effective date)
- Consent to Assign (CTA): TD SYNNEX will prepare a consolidated document with all payer IDs; Optum signs once; then a 10–15 minute call with both distributors' operations teams to transfer root access
- Important: If DSA is not moved in time, neither old nor new distributor receives the discount (street price applies)
- Canada: Rupesh remains account manager for both US and Canada regions; billing currency can be USD or CAD based on preference
- Former aggregator behavior: May attempt to retain the partner; TD SYNNEX noted competitors have dragged their feet in the past
 
New Customer Incentive (NCI)
NCI is a new program replacing POD (Partner Originated Discount), offering an additional 4% discount for 3 years on qualifying new accounts.
Status Details:
- Eligibility: Customer must have spent less than $50,000 in the prior 12 months (~$5,000/month) before linking
- Benefit: Extra 4% discount for 3 years (vs. POD's 5% for 2 years)
- Requirement: Customer must be enrolled in Billing Transfer
- Billing Transfer overview:
- Customer retains root access
- Handshake model (similar to Microsoft Azure CSP)
- Customer cannot see partner or distributor discounts
- Removes consent-to-assign requirement
- Reduces operational complexity and customer hesitation around giving up root
 
Bad Debt / Bankruptcy Protection
Optum raised concerns about customer bankruptcies and recovery options.
Status Details:
- Billing Transfer advantage: Partner can sever the handshake, stopping future liability; AWS holds remaining spend
- Infrastructure as a service is classified as a utility globally — cannot be shut off during bankruptcy protection
- TD SYNNEX process: Documents all spend, identifies legal counsel, petitions AWS for reimbursement on behalf of partners
- TD SYNNEX pays AWS every two weeks regardless of partner/customer payment status
- TD SYNNEX has successfully petitioned AWS to absorb losses in past cases
- TD SYNNEX noted as better positioned to absorb bad debt than individual partners
 
MDF (Market Development Funds)
TD SYNNEX offers an MDF match program tied to AWS MDF allocations.
Status Details:
- AWS MDF: Advanced partners with competency can access up to $50,000/year (typically ~$25,000); AWS covers 50% after proof of performance
- TD SYNNEX Match: Matches what AWS contributes (covering the other 50%)
- Allocation: Annual basis, not quarterly/monthly; based on qualification level and partner activity
- One-off events: Partners can request ad hoc MDF participation with a business case (ROI, cost, timing)
- TD SYNNEX distributes limited AWS funding across ~1,300–1,400 North America partners; more active partners get more access
 
Payment Terms
Payment terms are determined individually based on credit rating and financials.
Status Details:
- Process: TD SYNNEX credit team reviews financials during onboarding to establish credit line and terms
- Typical terms: Net 30 standard; Net 45 likely achievable; Net 90 rare (requires ~$2–4M/month in AWS spend)
- Terms cannot be confirmed in advance — financial review required first
- Christine to follow up offline on credit/payment term specifics

items.subject: Product delivery flow
items.body_text:
[Will Stevens]
Ok cool.  I was just making sure we were accounting for those details, which we clearly are.  Thanks team.

---

[Ben Kennedy]
None of this limits what we can do in Kirkland. In fact right now we don’t even have a single regular hosting rack. Once we repurpose MTC, at least we’ll have 2. I just don’t know the exact number that might be needed going forward

---

[George Revie]
Not an issue, there are racks planned for the deals you are referencing

---

[Will Stevens]
Or is this stuff "in prep for" better leveraging the space in Kirkland?

---

[Will Stevens]
I believe we have a couple deals in the works that are expecting to leverage the Kirkland DC (because it is a Canadian tier 3 facility). Does any of this limit the feasibility of that?

---

[Ben Kennedy]
This is more of an FYI for the group. But if anyone wants to weigh in, please do so. 

 

We need to remove our gear from the Kirkland MMRs. Not a big deal with no customers in Kirkland presently. While doing the necessary topology changes we were also going to do some other clean up. Specifically:

Remove network gear from PCI racks. There will be no PCI in Kirkland anymore. These were never setup as regular racks, so there are no Top-of-rack switches to repurpose. So at the moment these will be empty racks, network wise
Convert 2x MTC racks into regular 10G hosting racks. This will bring our count of (non-PCI) hosting racks with ready networking in Kirkland to: 2. I know there has been some more customer interest around Kirkland. But I haven't heard that there would need to be more than 2 racks sitting there waiting
After the topology changes are implemented our core network racks will remain as 1002.05 and 1002.06. But there will be space for any other internal gear, IE if shared storage is needed as part of some customer deal in Kirkland

---

[Martin Tessier]
Yep.. Aptum IaaS should take over..

---

[Will Stevens]
Again, we put our customers on ThinkOn's infra, but they are still our customers and we just changed where we are hosting our customer.  So I don't think it is technically a resell.  Anyway, we are getting down a rabbit hole.  All to say, we don't want to do the MTC route going forward.  We should be orienting around Aptum IaaS delivered through Apt Cloud as the ideal path (which supports both KVM and VMware at the hypervisor layer).

---

[Will Stevens]
I did a very high level review of what Cody was proposing for Technicost.  I asked him a bunch of questions and he was able to answer them.  I didn't do a full review of that RFP, but I did do a little bit of checking as a sniff test to make sure things were not totally off the rails, but I didn't fully vet or validate the solution being proposed.

---

[Martin Tessier]
Still .. our offering isn't What we use to call MTC.. our offering is a resale from TONS..

---

[Will Stevens]
We don't run MTC in our own DCs, but it is managed through Apt Cloud today on ThinkOn's infra.

---

[Will Stevens]
Thats not true.  It is exposed through Apt Cloud.  They are our customers (not thinkon's) even though they are running on ThinkOn's infra.  So we have to be careful because ThinkOn offers an MTC offering.  Our MTC offering for our customers depends on ThinkOn's offering.

---

[Adam Nelson]
It's for this: Technicost RFP - Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS).docx

---

[Martin Tessier]
what we use to call MTC doesn't exist anymore.. ..  it been migrated to ThinkOn MTC ( They have rebranded ThinkON VPC ) ..

---

[Will Stevens]
Martin Tessier is our MTC offering on top of ThinkOn MTC?

---

[Martin Tessier]
2 separate thing... ThinkOn provide our License for VMWAre ... and ThinkON MTC is a similar platform to what we are building with "Apt Cloud"  that run on VMWare..

---

[Adam Nelson]
I'm starting to think MA doesn't like Cody

---

[Adam Nelson]
There's apparently a call at noon today w/ Technicost and MA is not .... confident... in Cody's analysis of the situation

---

[Will Stevens]
I don't know if ThinkOn can provide us licenses to run in our own DCs.  Not sure

---

[Will Stevens]
I am not sure about the licensing piece

---

[Adam Nelson]
man, I feel so stupid

---

[Adam Nelson]
like get a license from thinkon for vmware vs. going w/ TO MTC?

---

[Adam Nelson]
ok, so we can provide vmware outside of thinkon's mtc?

---

[Will Stevens]
So the answer is yes, but it depends on how VMware is setup and managed, etc...

---

[Will Stevens]
Adam Nelson assuming they want VMware, yes.  However, there is a nuance to that answer.

Apt Cloud supports VMware Cloud Director natively, but with limited support.  It is a much more complicated implementation because it depends on a few different VMware components (VRops, vRealize, etc).  VMware is prone to issues in not being consistent with their usage data.  All to say, it is possible.  This is how MTC is exposed through Apt Cloud today.  We can expose self-service, but for MTC, we haven't exposed self-service to our customers (too many competing priorities).
CloudStack supports VMware as a hypervisor (at the vSphere API layer) out of the box and it is arguably the best supported hypervisor (from a feature perspective).  This means that the Aptum IaaS layer can support VMware clusters being orchestrated through that implementation.  We can dedicate clusters to customers within the Aptum IaaS layer if we need to.  So a customer would then be able to orchestrate shared resources (on our KVM infra) and also orchestrate their down dedicated VMware resources through the same interface.

---

[Adam Nelson]
The customer is w/ Leaseweb on a VMWare MTC

---

[Adam Nelson]
Martin Tessier Will Stevens - For technicost, are we able to use VMWare w/ Aptcloud? (I don't know what I'm talking about)

---

[Ben Kennedy]
Yup, makes sense

---

[Adam Nelson]
I'm trying to figure out (distilled to as simple as i can think of it)... "what did we buy that we can sell, what of that did we sell, what's left?" on the PS side it's people & hours, on compute side it's servers, on colo side it's space & power

---

[Will Stevens]
The other two are SCADACore and Alberta New Home Warranty Program

---

[Will Stevens]
ES Williams is 5 of the 7, but as separate customers.

---

[Ben Kennedy]
We have X capacity in connectivity (bandwidth, cross-connects, transit/peering) and infrastructure devices (switches, firewalls, load balancers)
We've sold/deployed Y to customers and current operations
We have Z capacity available to sell or deploy without spending more

comments from me: I'd say this is mostly right. But for x and z, I mean it would be like x1, x2, etc and z1, z2, etc. Given the number of different capacity elements.

Firewalls and load balancers are tricky for X. We have inventory of firewalls, so you could call that X capacity. For load balancers we are at Citrix's whims. Traditionally it's an incremental cost, not a fixed cost for X capacity. This year we are use a loophole to only pay for 5 licenses, 1 of which is licensing all our managed hosting load balancers as that license gives as access to a 1TB pool. So this year we could call that "x" capacity

---

[Ben Kennedy]
"do capitalized hours happen in non-project specific contexts?" - For me team, no

 

" To me it would make sense that if work is being capitalized, then a dedicated jira project would be setup to do that work.  Is that a fair assumption?" - As long as we can create some link between the jira project used for the "regular" work and the jira project used for the "capitalized" work, then this could work

---

I mean, isn't ES Williams technically 1? or are we counting them as 7

oof,,, I wish you didn't take screenshots haha

Last year, you were tracking Technology Services trends. So here’s what you need to know now: AI Economics™ is dismantling seat-based SaaS growth—and forcing a shift to outcome delivery where services become the profit engine.
The State of Technology Services 2026 breaks down what’s actually changing—and what AI-native leaders are doing differently. It’s not opinion-based. It’s TSIA research mapping how the market is splitting right now.

  

Inside, you'll see: 
Why services, not software or tech alone, will define the AI-era winners.
What’s actually blocking scale in the last mile (data, integration, governance, and talent).

AI isn’t eliminating technology services. It’s making them indispensible—because outcomes don’t deliver themselves.

Best,
George Humphrey

Yup accurate although a bit detailed for the current audience but very good. So here is what I think we probably need to align around: https://aptum.com/services/ and this: https://aptum.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/Product/overview - if our public facing and internal facing docs don't align that is an issue. Also we need to get ahead in the GSE process of (a) I need lego blocks to add to my existing kit (b) I need a new lego kit (c) I want a lego "city" (custom or multiple integrated kits).

---

[Adam Nelson]
3. Product Definitions
3.1 Apt Cloud (The Platform)

"Apt" conveys capability, precision, and readiness — a cloud that is tailored to needs, not one-size-fits-all. Apt Cloud ties directly to Aptum's identity as a hybrid cloud managed services provider.

Apt Cloud is Aptum's next-generation cloud operations platform, accessed at portal.aptum.com. It is a white-labeled instance of CloudOps Software that acts as the single control plane for Aptum-delivered cloud services.

What it delivers:

Self-service provisioning (VMs, networks, storage, DNS)
Role-based access control (RBAC) with granular permissions
Cost and usage visibility with real-time cost estimator
Lifecycle management (create, scale, snapshot, decommission)
Activity logging (visible activity logs across all services; note: not traditional monitoring/push alerts at this layer today)
Unified governance across private and hyperscale clouds
Integration with Aptum managed services workflows
Multi-tenant organizations with white-label branding (per-reseller)
Full REST API (API-first design)
Monetization engine: Product catalogs, pricing, commitments, utility pricing, revenue reporting, invoicing, discounts, credits, CC integration, tax integration (bold = already set up; italic = available, could be using)
Link to support / ticketing system (planned)

What it is NOT:

Apt Cloud does NOT provide physical infrastructure
Apt Cloud does NOT replace hyperscale provider consoles (Azure Portal, AWS Console)
Apt Cloud does NOT bypass managed services engagement

Services available within Apt Cloud (portal.aptum.com) today:

Aptum IaaS — VMs on CloudStack (new)
MTC — VMs on ThinkOn / VMware Cloud Director (legacy MTC customers)
Microsoft Azure — Instances, disks, networks, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
Cloudflare DNS — Domains and DNS records (replaces SuperDNS; built and plan developed, rollout pending customer communication)
Available to adopt (functionality built, not yet configured): AWS, GCP, Kubernetes (standalone)
3.2 Aptum IaaS (The Infrastructure)

Aptum IaaS consists of compute, storage, and networking services running on Aptum-owned hardware in Aptum data centres, orchestrated by Apache CloudStack. It is delivered in two models:

Virtual Private Cloud (VPC):

Multi-tenant shared compute infrastructure
Logical isolation with private networking (VLANs; VXLAN support TBD — needs validation with KVM implementation)
Dedicated resource quotas
Predictable performance within a pooled system

Private Cloud:

Single-tenant dedicated compute nodes
Enhanced security, compliance, and customization
Custom networking and storage configurations
Same operational model and automation layer as VPC
Combines technical infrastructure with managed services (people + technology)
Note: Bare Metal as a Service (BMaaS) integration is not yet implemented. The platform has a 5c/Hypertec BMaaS integration (Canonical MaaS at the CloudStack layer, built by CloudOps Inc) which could be leveraged. Currently, "Private Cloud" means dedicated physical hosts delivered through the virtual orchestration layer — not direct bare metal provisioning.

---

[Ian Rae]
We will be in the basement

---

[Ian Rae]
Hi popping out to get some stuff. Entrance is next to garage door in driveway if you get here early door is open .

Bonjour Adam,  

Sous nos pieds, un réseau invisible tient nos villes en vie. Aqueducs centenaires, tunnels de métro qui s'effritent, voirie qui cède sous le poids des hivers : les infrastructures municipales québécoises sont à la croisée des chemins. Les besoins dépassent les capacités, les budgets sont contraints, et attendre n'est plus une option.

Des solutions existent pourtant. Le 22 avril prochain, notre conférence Réinventer la ville : infrastructures réunira décideur·euses, ingénieur·es et expert·es pour partager des approches concrètes : financer autrement, prioriser intelligemment, moderniser sans tout recommencer.

Une journée dense, tournée vers l'action, avec des cas réels venus de Montréal, du Québec et même de Belgique.

Parce que rebâtir nos villes commence par savoir exactement où elles en sont.

À travers des innovations techniques et des stratégies concrètes, ils et elles mettront en lumière comment mieux planifier, réaliser et financer des infrastructures municipales durables et performantes, tout en misant sur une collaboration étroite entre acteurs publics et partenaires privés.

Une analyse stratégique des infrastructures urbaines
Des panels d’expert·es sur les innovations techniques et financières
